does: This paper expands consistency training, a technique that encourages models to maintain consistent behavior across contexts, by introducing two new internal consistency targets operating at different transformer layers. MLP Consistency Training matches post-activation states within feedforward networks, while Attention Consistency Training matches per-head attention distributions. The authors apply these methods to four safety threats beyond prior work: persona in-context learning attacks, adversarial frustration, prefill attacks, and conditional misalignment. They demonstrate across multiple models that these consistency training variants reduce misalignment in these diverse threat settings, document cross-threat generalization where training against one failure mode improves robustness to others, and mechanistically analyze how these methods share underlying residual-stream mechanisms while differing from earlier behavior consistency training approaches.
connects: This work builds on prior consistency training research that originally focused on reducing sycophancy and jailbreaking through matching model outputs across semantically equivalent contexts. The paper extends this line by targeting internal model states rather than just final outputs, drawing on mechanistic interpretability work that examines how information flows through transformer layers. It connects to broader safety research addressing various forms of misalignment and adversarial vulnerabilities in language models, including work on in-context learning vulnerabilities and adversarial robustness. The mechanistic analysis linking multiple consistency training variants through residual-stream mechanisms reflects growing understanding of how safety interventions operate within transformer architectures, building on recent advances in understanding transformer circuits and layer-wise behavior.
enables: This work opens directions for developing more targeted safety interventions by clarifying which internal mechanisms drive different failure modes and how consistency training can be tuned to specific vulnerabilities. The cross-threat generalization findings suggest that safety improvements may transfer across seemingly unrelated misalignment types, potentially leading to more efficient safety training protocols. The mechanistic framework distinguishing between residual-stream and other-based consistency mechanisms enables future work to design layer-specific interventions optimized for particular safety problems. The results point toward safety approaches that leverage internal model structure rather than operating only at the output level, supporting the development of interpretability-guided alignment techniques that could scale to larger models and more complex safety threats.
